Thinking, Fast and Slow|Daniel Kahneman.description: The foundational work on cognitive biases and dual-process theory.

The Color of Law|Richard Rothstein.description: How government policy created residential segregation in America.

Sapiens|Yuval Noah Harari.description: A sweeping history of humankind from the Stone Age to Silicon Valley.

Homo Deus|Yuval Noah Harari.description: Where humanity is headed next — from Homo sapiens to technological gods.

The Guns of August|Barbara Tuchman.description: The definitive account of the first month of World War I and the failures of diplomacy.

Capital in the Twenty-First Century|Thom.description: The landmark study of wealth inequality and its deep historical roots.

Evicted|Matthew Desmond.description: Pulitzer-winning account of poverty, housing, and eviction in America.

The Power Broker|Robert Caro.description: The epic biography of Robert Moses and the making of modern New York.

The Alignment Problem|Brian Christian.description: How machine learning reflects and amplifies human values — and where it goes wrong.

Superintelligence|Nick Bostrom.description: The canonical argument for why advanced AI poses existential risk.

The Structure of Scientific Revolutions|.description: The book that gave us 'paradigm shift' — how science actually changes.

Manufacturing Consent|Noam Chomsky.description: The propaganda model of mass media and how institutional filters shape the news.

The Origins of Totalitarianism|Hannah Ar.description: Arendt's analysis of how totalitarian movements arise from loneliness and terror.

On Tyranny|Timothy Snyder.description: Twenty lessons from the twentieth century on resisting authoritarianism.

Why Nations Fail|Daron Acemoglu.description: How political and economic institutions determine why some countries thrive and others don't.

The Righteous Mind|Jonathan Haidt.description: Why good people are divided by politics and religion — moral psychology explained.

Bowling Alone|Robert Putnam.description: The collapse and revival of American community — why social capital matters.

The Wealth of Nations|Adam Smith.description: The foundational text of modern economics and free market theory.

The Art of War|Sun Tzu.description: The ancient Chinese treatise on strategy, still applied in business and conflict.

1984|George Orwell.description: The definitive novel of surveillance, propaganda, and totalitarian control.

A Theory of Justice|John Rawls.description: The most influential work of political philosophy in the twentieth century.

The Road to Serfdom|Friedrich Hayek.description: Hayek's warning that central planning leads inevitably to authoritarianism.

The Clash of Civilizations|Samuel Huntin.description: The controversial thesis that cultural identity drives post-Cold War conflict.

Caste|Isabel Wilkerson.description: America's hidden caste system — connecting race in America to caste worldwide.

Life 3.0|Max Tegmark.description: What it means to be human in the age of artificial intelligence.

Human Compatible|Stuart Russell.description: How to build AI that is provably beneficial — from one of the field's founders.

Nudge|Richard Thaler.description: How small design choices in how options are presented can dramatically improve decisions.

Factfulness|Hans Rosling.description: Ten instincts that distort our worldview — and why the world is better than you think.

The Innovator's Dilemma|Clayton Christen.description: Why great companies fail when disruptive technologies emerge.

Zero to One|Peter Thiel.description: Thiel's contrarian guide to building startups that create something genuinely new.

The Lean Startup|Eric Ries.description: Build-measure-learn — the methodology that changed how startups operate.

Atomic Habits|James Clear.description: Small changes, remarkable results — the science of habit formation.

Deep Work|Cal Newport.description: Rules for focused success in a distracted world.

The Age of AI|Henry Kissinger.description: How artificial intelligence will reshape security, politics, and human identity.

Gödel, Escher, Bach|Douglas Hofstadter.description: A Pulitzer-winning exploration of consciousness, formal systems, and the strange loops connecting math, art, and music.

The Age of Surveillance Capitalism|Shosh.description: How tech companies turned human experience into raw material for prediction and control.

The Diary of a Young Girl|Anne Frank.description: The intimate journal of a Jewish girl hiding from the Nazis — humanity's most personal Holocaust testimony.

Thinking in Systems|Donella Meadows.description: A primer on systems thinking — how to see the interconnections that drive complex behavior.

Too Big to Fail|Andrew Ross Sorkin.description: The definitive inside account of the 2008 financial crisis and the people who caused it.

The Big Short|Michael Lewis.description: How a handful of investors saw the housing bubble and bet against Wall Street.

A Beautiful Mind|Sylvia Nasar.description: The biography of John Nash — mathematical genius, Nobel laureate, and his struggle with schizophrenia.

The Design of Everyday Things|Don Norman.description: Why some designs work and others don't — the psychology of good design.

Clean Code|Robert Martin.description: A handbook of agile software craftsmanship — writing code that humans can read and maintain.

Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approa.description: The standard textbook on AI — comprehensive, authoritative, and used in universities worldwide.

The End of Alchemy|Mervyn King.description: A former central banker explains why the global financial system remains dangerously fragile.

The Great War for Civilisation|Robert Fi.description: Fisk's magisterial account of the Middle East's wars from the perspective of those who lived through them.

The Ascent of Money|Niall Ferguson.description: A financial history of the world — how money, credit, and banking shaped civilization.

The Looming Tower|Lawrence Wright.description: Pulitzer-winning account of the road to 9/11 — al-Qaeda, the FBI, and the intelligence failures.

The End of History and the Last Man|Fran.description: Fukuyama's famous thesis that liberal democracy is the final form of government.

The Great Influenza|John M. Barry.description: The epic story of the 1918 pandemic — the deadliest plague in history and the scientists who fought it.

Spillover|David Quammen.description: How animal infections jump to humans — the science of the next pandemic, written before COVID.

The Hot Zone|Richard Preston.description: The terrifying true story of Ebola's emergence — a thriller that reads like fiction but isn't.

The Beatles|Bob Spitz.description: The definitive biography of the Beatles — exhaustive, intimate, and authoritative.

Design Patterns|Erich Gamma.description: The Gang of Four book — 23 reusable design patterns that changed how software is built.

1776|David McCullough.description: The riveting account of the year that decided America's fate — from the siege of Boston to the crossing of the Delaware.

Cycles of Time|Roger Penrose.description: Penrose's radical proposal that the universe cycles through infinite aeons — conformal cyclic cosmology explained.

How Buildings Learn|Stewart Brand.description: What happens after buildings are built — how they adapt, evolve, and why some survive while others don't.

California Crackup|Joe Mathews.description: How ballot initiatives, term limits, and structural dysfunction broke California's governance.

The Population Bomb|Paul Ehrlich.description: The 1968 bestseller that predicted mass famine — spectacularly wrong, yet its ideas still shape environmentalism.

Moby-Dick|Herman Melville.description: The great American novel of obsession, meaning, and the sea — still unmatched after 170 years.

Kill Chain|Andrew Cockburn.description: How drone warfare and high-tech assassination became central to American military strategy.

Drift|Rachel Maddow.description: How America drifted into a state of permanent war without the public noticing or consenting.

Beyond Good and Evil|Friedrich Nietzsche.description: Nietzsche's assault on conventional morality — arguing that hatred, envy, and power are essential to life.

Silent Spring|Rachel Carson.description: The book that launched the environmental movement — exposing how pesticides were poisoning the natural world.

The Closing of the American Mind|Allan B.description: Bloom's controversial argument that higher education has failed democracy and impoverished students' souls.

The Gilded Age|Mark Twain.description: The satirical novel that gave an era its name — Twain and Warner skewering post-Civil War greed and corruption.

Lifespan|David Sinclair.description: A Harvard geneticist argues aging is a disease we can treat — and lays out the science for doing it.

The Music Lesson|Victor Wooten.description: A bass legend teaches music through story — rhythm, space, and listening as spiritual practice.

Lyrical Ballads|William Wordsworth.description: The slim 1798 volume that launched English Romanticism — Wordsworth and Coleridge redefining what poetry could be.

The Prelude|William Wordsworth.description: Wordsworth's autobiographical epic — the growth of a poet's mind across four landmark texts.

Sense and Sensibility|Jane Austen.description: Austen's first published novel — two sisters navigating love, money, and society with contrasting temperaments.

Capital|Karl Marx.description: Marx's foundational critique of political economy — how capitalism produces and reproduces its own contradictions.

Body by Science|Doug McGuff.description: The research-based case for high-intensity, single-set strength training — maximum results in minimum time.

Religion and Nothingness|Keiji Nishitani.description: The Kyoto School philosopher on emptiness, nihilism, and what religion actually is beyond Western utility.

Jesus the Magician|Morton Smith.description: A historian reveals how Jesus was viewed by contemporaries — miracle worker to allies, sorcerer to enemies.

Signals|Brian Skyrms.description: How meaningful communication evolves from mindless processes — signaling without intelligence.

Doppelganger|Naomi Klein.description: Klein explores mirror worlds, conspiracy culture, and the strange doublings of modern politics.

After Virtue|Alasdair MacIntyre.description: Why modern moral debates feel hollow — we inherited the language of ethics without the tradition that gave it meaning.

Varieties of Religion Today|Charles Tayl.description: Taylor revisits William James to ask what religion means in a secular age — personal experience meets social architecture.

The Trial|Franz Kafka.description: Kafka's nightmare of bureaucratic persecution — Joseph K is arrested, tried, and condemned by a system he can never understand.

Animal Farm|George Orwell.description: Orwell's allegory of revolution betrayed — the animals overthrow the farmer, then become worse than what they replaced.

The Rebel|Albert Camus.description: Camus's devastating critique of revolutionary violence — when rebellion loses its limits, it becomes tyranny.

The Plague|Albert Camus.description: A town besieged by plague becomes Camus's image of solidarity in the face of absurdity.

The Stranger|Albert Camus.description: Meursault kills an Arab on an Algerian beach — and refuses to pretend he feels what society demands.

2010: Odyssey Two|Arthur C. Clarke.description: Clarke's sequel to 2001 — a mission to Jupiter's moons and the discovery of alien life on Europa.

Notes from Underground|Fyodor Dostoevsky.description: The underground man rages against rationalism — Dostoevsky's first great portrait of nihilism and self-sabotage.

Crime and Punishment|Fyodor Dostoevsky.description: Raskolnikov murders a pawnbroker to prove he's extraordinary — then discovers he's not.

Tragedy, the Greeks, and Us|Simon Critch.description: Why Greek tragedy still matters — ambiguity, fragility, and what modern philosophy has lost.

Frankenstein|Mary Shelley.description: The Modern Prometheus — a teenager's warning about science without ethics that launched an entire genre.

Verbal Behavior|B.F. Skinner.description: Skinner's behaviorist account of language — the book Chomsky demolished in the most famous review in intellectual history.

The Philosophy of Zen Buddhism|Byung-Chu.description: Han peels back Western selfhood to reveal what Zen offers — emptiness, dwelling nowhere, friendliness.

The Burnout Society|Byung-Chul Han.description: How neoliberal self-optimization turned us into exhausted entrepreneurs of ourselves.

Four Quartets|T.S. Eliot.description: Eliot's late masterpiece — time, memory, and the still point of the turning world.

The Iliad|Homer.description: The foundational poem of Western literature — Achilles, Hector, and the brutal poetry of war.

Songs of Innocence and Experience|Willia.description: Blake's twin visions of childhood and corruption — the divine imagination against the fallen world.

Build a Large Language Model (From Scrat.description: Hands-on guide to building an LLM from the ground up — attention, training, and fine-tuning explained through code.

Towards a New Architecture|Le Corbusier.description: The manifesto that redefined modern architecture — form follows function, buildings as machines for living.

The Gambler|Fyodor Dostoevsky.description: Dostoevsky wrote himself out of debt with this raw study of addiction — one more spin, always one more spin.

With the Old Breed|Eugene Sledge.description: The most honest account of Pacific combat ever written — Peleliu and Okinawa through a Marine's eyes.

The Bomber Mafia|Malcolm Gladwell.description: How a group of idealists tried to make war humane through precision bombing — and ended up burning down Japan.
